turns.text: Hello Brinda, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're thinking about starting a family, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

It’s funny, I’ve sat on the other side of this conversation a thousand times in the pediatric ward. I’m usually the one holding the clipboard, explaining the "why" behind a vaccine schedule or the necessity of a screening. But sitting here now, trying to prepare my body for a second child at thirty-seven, the roles feel… inverted. 

My husband and I are so ready for this. We want our daughter to have a sibling to grow up with here, to run around near the water in Wareham just like she does now. But I’ll be honest with you—it’s exhausting to know too much. When I’m at my OB’s office, I’m trying to be a patient, but my brain keeps defaulting to "peer review" mode. I start thinking about uterine perfusion or chromosomal risks before she’s even finished her sentence. 

I want to be the mother who just stares at a tiny hand and feels wonder, but I’m terrified I’ll just be checking capillary refill and monitoring for every minor distress sign. It’s hard to switch off the provider brain when that’s how you’ve defined yourself for years. I’m hopeful, truly, but I’m also holding my breath. It’s a strange place to be—prepared for everything, yet feeling completely vulnerable. Does that make sense?

Before you started trying to conceive, what did you expect the process of getting support or guidance from healthcare professionals to look like? And thinking back now, what did that support actually look like in reality?

I think I expected a more... guided experience? Maybe I was naive, but I thought being a clinician myself would act as a kind of shorthand. I imagined that when I walked into the office, we’d skip the basic brochures and go straight to the nuanced, high-level stuff. I wanted someone to hold space for the fact that I already understand the biology, so we could focus on the emotional hurdles—the things you can’t look up in a textbook.

In reality, it’s felt like a balancing act. My OB is truly wonderful, and she’s incredibly patient, but the dynamic is awkward. She’s trying to be gentle, and I’m trying not to interrupt her with my own clinical interpretation of our conversation. When she tells me I’m low-risk, I hear her, but then I spend the rest of the night spiraling about the "what-ifs" that I’ve seen in my own career. 

There’s this unspoken gap between the clinical "low risk" label and the internal reality of being thirty-seven. I haven’t really found someone to talk to about that dissonance—the fear that my professional expertise is actually a barrier to just being a patient. I wanted a partner in this, but often, I just feel like I'm managing my own case file.
Before trying to conceive, what did you expect your own emotional readiness to feel like? And how did your emotional state actually compare to those expectations once the process began?

I suppose I expected emotional readiness to feel like a steady, calm confidence. Because I work with newborns and parents every day, I convinced myself that I’d be "inoculated" against the typical anxieties. I thought I would be the patient who was totally Zen, someone who knew exactly what to look for and therefore had nothing to fear. I imagined myself being the anchor for my husband, gracefully navigating the biological milestones with clinical precision.

But the reality? It’s been far more jagged. I didn’t anticipate that my professional knowledge would turn into a kind of psychic toll. It’s not a steady hum of calm; it’s more like a constant, low-grade static in the back of my mind. Every time I counsel a parent about Tdap boosters or explain the importance of maternal antibodies, I feel that prick of anxiety in my own arm. It’s impossible to turn off the "provider" part of my brain, and that has actually made me feel *less* protected, not more. I thought I’d be in control, but I feel incredibly vulnerable, just waiting for the heartbeat. It’s humbling, in a really terrifying way, to realize that being an expert doesn’t make you immune to the fear of the unknown.
What was missing from the support available to you emotionally at this stage — from professionals, your partner, or your wider network?

I think what’s been missing is a space where I’m allowed to *not* be the expert. 

When I’m with my colleagues or even my OB, there’s this unspoken assumption that because I have the credentials, I have the coping mechanisms. It’s almost like they think I’ve already synthesized all the "what-ifs" I’ve seen in my career into some kind of armor. But I haven’t. I’m just as scared of a complication as any other thirty-seven-year-old mother. I desperately need someone to look past the medical jargon and just ask, "How are *you* doing with this weight?" without expecting me to analyze my own stress levels like a lab report.

My husband is wonderful, but he’s in the thick of it with me—he’s just as anxious, just in a different way. We end up feeding off each other’s hyper-vigilance instead of grounding one another. I find myself wishing for a support system that acknowledges that a medical background doesn't make you a super-human patient; it actually makes you more prone to over-analyzing every twinge. I need someone to tell me it’s okay to put down the stethoscope and just be a woman who is trying to bring a life into the world, rather than a clinician constantly evaluating the risks of the process.
Before conception, what did you expect to find when you looked for information online or through apps about fertility and getting pregnant? And what did you actually find — was it what you needed?

Honestly, I think I expected the information to be either overly simplistic or just plain wrong. I’m used to navigating peer-reviewed journals and clinical databases, so when I first downloaded those mainstream pregnancy apps, I was almost annoyed by the tone. They’re so bright and breezy—all flowery emojis and "baby is the size of a blueberry" analogies—that it felt almost condescending. They treat pregnancy like this uncomplicated, magical event, which, for someone who has seen the pediatric ICU, feels like a massive omission of reality.

What I actually found was a desert of nuance. I’d be searching for data on advanced maternal age, and the apps would give me generic "don't worry, you're fine!" platitudes that felt dismissive. They don't account for the "provider brain." They don't have a toggle setting for "I’m a clinician, skip the fluff and show me the data." 

It’s been frustrating. I either get the dumbed-down version that leaves me feeling like they’re hiding the risks, or I fall down a rabbit hole of medical literature that just spikes my cortisol levels. I never found that middle ground—a source that is scientifically rigorous but also emotionally honest about the fear that comes with being thirty-seven. I don't need a cartoon baby; I need someone to hold space for the clinical *and* the human.
If you had a pre-existing health condition before pregnancy, what did you expect the healthcare system to do to help you prepare? And what actually happened when you sought that help?

I don't have a chronic illness in the traditional sense, but in my own mind, my age feels like a "pre-existing condition." Before we started trying, I fully expected my OB to sit me down for a structured, proactive preconception visit—a comprehensive roadmap that acknowledged the statistical realities of being thirty-seven while validating my specific concerns. I wanted a space to talk through the "what-ifs" with someone who wouldn't just dismiss them as "not a problem yet."

In reality, the system is just not built for that level of preventative mental space. When I brought it up, the response was very much, "You're healthy, everything looks normal, just start trying and let us know." It felt like a standard protocol check-off. Because my baseline vitals are fine and I’m technically low-risk, the system essentially gave me a pat on the back and sent me on my way. 

It was frustrating because I felt like I was being treated as a statistic rather than a person with a specific, heightened level of anxiety. I didn't want to be told I was "fine"; I wanted a plan for how to manage the uncertainty. I walked out of that office feeling like I was essentially on my own to navigate the emotional labor of the wait. It’s hard to feel supported when the medical consensus is simply to "wait and see."
Did you feel you had to advocate strongly for yourself, or did the system proactively reach out to support you?

Oh, it was definitely not proactive. The system, at least in my experience, is built to respond to pathology, not to the pre-emptive emotional needs of a woman who knows too much. If you aren't currently presenting with a complication or a clear, measurable deviation from the norm, you’re essentially "invisible" until you’re officially pregnant. 

I felt like I had to perform a delicate dance. If I asked too many specific, data-heavy questions, I could see the shift in the room—a subtle tightening of the provider’s posture because they’re suddenly talking to a peer instead of a standard patient. It felt like I had to "prove" I was allowed to be worried, or else I was just being brushed off as a neurotic physician. I had to push to be taken seriously about my concerns, almost like I was fighting for a level of validation that shouldn't have been up for negotiation. 

It makes you feel incredibly lonely. You’re navigating these internal minefields, trying to find someone who will act as a partner, but instead, you find yourself essentially managing your own care trajectory. I didn't want to have to advocate for empathy or for a nuanced conversation, but I realized very quickly that if I didn't initiate that, I was just going to be treated like any other low-risk file.
Before you conceived, what did you expect your partner's involvement in the preconception process to look like? And how did their actual involvement compare to what you'd imagined?

I imagined us as this perfectly synchronized team. I thought we’d be reading the same books, discussing nutrition and lifestyle changes over dinner, and that he would be my primary sounding board for all the fears I didn't want to bring into the hospital. I pictured him being the one to gently remind me to breathe when I’d start obsessing over maternal-fetal statistics.

The reality has been much more complicated. He’s incredibly supportive, but he’s also navigating this from a completely different vantage point. He doesn't have the "provider brain." He doesn't walk into a room and immediately spot the clinical markers of risk, so when I try to explain *why* I’m spiraling over a specific study, he sees it as me being pessimistic or over-analyzing. It’s hard because he wants to be the "everything is going to be fine" guy, and I crave that comfort, but part of me also feels frustrated that he *isn’t* as worried as I am. 

I’ve had to learn that his version of involvement is more about "doing"—he handles the logistics, he’s there for the appointments, he makes sure I’m eating well—but the emotional burden of the "what-ifs" remains largely a solitary confinement. I love him, but I’ve realized he can’t be the one to bridge the gap between my clinical knowledge and my maternal anxiety. That’s a distance I’m still figuring out how to cross on my own.
Were there moments where you felt the burden of preparation fell disproportionately on you? What drove that?

It’s been almost entirely on my shoulders, and it’s exhausting. The burden of preparation—the nutrition, the supplements, the mental gymnastics of trying to create the "perfect environment" for a pregnancy—is overwhelmingly mine. 

I think what drives it is a mix of biology and my own conditioning. I’m the one whose body has to do the heavy lifting, and my professional background keeps whispering that if I don’t optimize every single variable, I’m failing. There’s a specific kind of arrogance in thinking you can control biological outcomes if you just try hard enough, and I’m definitely guilty of that. I see my husband trying to help, but he doesn't have the same visceral, cellular-level pressure that I do. He’s supporting me, but I’m the one *being* the vessel. 

There’s this unspoken cultural script, too—even in a medical marriage—where the "maternal duty" begins at the intention phase. I catch myself feeling resentful that I’m the one researching everything, tracking my cycle with clinical precision, and internalizing all the potential complications, while he’s just waiting for the green light. It’s a lonely kind of labor. I’m preparing for a child, but I feel like I’m also preparing for a war I’m fighting mostly by myself.
If you experienced a previous pregnancy loss, what did you expect the healthcare system to offer you when you decided to try again? And what support was actually available to you?

I haven't experienced a loss, though in my line of work, I see the aftermath of it constantly. Perhaps that’s exactly why the fear feels so sharp. I’m not just preparing for a baby; I’m preparing for the possibility of heartbreak that I’ve witnessed so many other families endure. It’s like I’ve been given a front-row seat to every complication that could possibly happen, and now I’m stepping into the ring myself.

Because I haven’t had a loss, the system doesn’t treat me with that extra layer of sensitivity. I don't get the "high-risk" follow-up or the extra check-ins. I’m just "low risk," which in my world is a good thing, but in my heart, it feels like I’m walking around with a giant, invisible target on my back. 

I expected that someone—a midwife, a doctor, a counselor—would sit me down and help me build a mental health "care plan" alongside the physical one. I wanted someone to say, "Brinda, we know what you do for a living, and we know this makes your anxiety unique. Here is how we are going to support you when you inevitably start to panic." Instead, I’m just another chart. The support is entirely physical, entirely clinical, and entirely blind to the weight of what I’m carrying in my head.
Was there a point where you felt your previous loss was not being taken seriously enough in the context of your new pregnancy attempt?

I think there might be a slight misunderstanding—I haven't personally experienced a pregnancy loss, but that’s almost the source of the friction. I’ve spent my entire career witnessing the losses of *others*, and the trauma of those stories lives in me. It’s like secondary PTSD. 

When I mention my fears to my OB or other providers, I feel like they look at my chart, see "G1, P1" or however it's documented, and because I don't have a history of loss on my *own* record, they assume I shouldn't be anxious. It’s dismissed as "first-time jitters" or just the normal nervousness of being an older mom. They don't seem to grasp that my perspective isn't coming from a place of ignorance, but from a place of too much information. 

I’m treated as if my innocence is still intact, but it’s not. I’ve seen the rare complications, the heartbreaks that don't make it into the "low-risk" statistics. When I try to voice that, I feel like the system is waiting for me to actually suffer a loss before they’ll give me the kind of reassurance or extra monitoring I’m craving. It’s as if they need a "proven" vulnerability to take my emotional state seriously. It’s incredibly isolating to feel like you’re waiting for a tragedy to happen just to be validated in your fear.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think I’ve said a lot, but if I’m being honest, there’s this lingering hope—and maybe it’s a bit of a plea—that we could start viewing maternal care as a holistic partnership rather than just a clinical protocol. 

It isn't just about the ultrasound pictures or the blood pressure readings. It’s about recognizing that every woman who walks through that door brings her own life, her own traumas, and yes, even her own "baggage" of medical knowledge, into that room with her. I don’t want to be a patient who has to censor my own fears because I’m worried about coming off as "difficult" or "too clinical." 

I just want to get to a point where I can be vulnerable without feeling like I’m losing my agency. I want to move toward a version of care where the provider sees the *person* behind the chart—where they might look at me and say, "I know you know the stats, but let's talk about the *feeling* of this." 

It’s a small shift, really, but it would change everything for me. It would mean I could finally stop being the provider for one moment and just be a mother-to-be who is allowed to be human, to be messy, and to be uncertain. That’s all I’m really looking for. Thank you for listening to me today; it actually feels a bit lighter having said all that out loud.
